intro:
Moriah appears in two significant OT passages. In Genesis 22:2 God commands Abraham to take Isaac 'to the region of Moriah' and offer him there as a burnt offering 'on one of the mountains I will tell you about' — the binding of Isaac (Akedah). The narrative does not further identify the region beyond its three-day journey from Beersheba. In 2 Chronicles 3:1 Solomon begins to build the temple 'on Mount Moriah, where the LORD had appeared to his father David, on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite' — the site David had purchased to offer sacrifice after the plague (2 Sam. 24:18-25). This Chronicles verse links the specific mountain of the temple to the region of the Akedah, a connection that has profoundly shaped Jewish and Christian interpretation.

Jewish tradition extensively developed the identification of the Akedah site with the temple mount, making Mount Moriah the single location where Abraham's offering, David's purchase, Solomon's temple, and ultimately God's own sacrifice of his Son converge in Christian reading. Jerusalem's temple mount (now the site of the Dome of the Rock) is identified by this tradition as the precise location of the Akedah. ISBE notes that while the identification of the Genesis 22 site with the Jerusalem temple mount involves traditional rather than certain historical verification, the convergence of the Abraham-Isaac narrative with the later sanctuary tradition is theologically significant: both involve a divinely appointed mountain, a divinely provided substitute, and an altar representing humanity's covenant relationship with God. The name Moriah's association with 'seeing/appearing' — 'the LORD will provide' (Gen. 22:14) — adds to its theological resonance as the place of divine provision.
